chapters.title: Freedom is the basis of Ukrainian philosophy
chapters.text:
The Ukrainian literature curriculum began in the 1700s with philosopher Hryhoriy Skovoroda, who traveled through eastern Ukraine formulating ideas to inspire the youth. Many students find strength at Skovoroda's monument; the president and students at Kyiv-Mohyla Academy clean the memorial annually.

Skovoroda did not seek to create any philosophical system. After graduating from the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, he traveled Europe for several years, and after returning to Ukraine, he pursued teaching but soon resigned. The management did not like his innovative approach, and Skovoroda disagreed with the contemporary teaching methods. Later, he chose an ascetic lifestyle, devoting time to traveling and writing fables, philosophical dialogues, and treatises.

He conveyed the idea of freedom in his poem De Libertate, as freedom and self-knowledge were his central values. In search of happiness, Skovoroda concluded that people could be happy when they embraced themselves and their vocation.

Skovoroda said gold is a pile of mud compared to freedom.

While Skovoroda was traveling to Sloboda, Ukraine, Ivan Kotliarevsky, a titan of new Ukrainian literature, studied and worked in nearby Poltava. He wrote his most famous text, Eneida,in colloquial Ukrainian. Eneida is a poem that parodies Virgil's Aeneid, and Kotliarevsky wrote it to highlight Ukrainian social issues. It is a hilarious and lively story about fiery Ukrainian soldiers led by Aeneas.

Eneida is a perfect example of the “live” Ukrainian language. There are 7,000 words in the text, including household items and traditional Ukrainian rituals. Kotliarevsky dared to be frivolous in his interpretation and used many idioms and sayings. Despite its age, his poem still rings true.

With monuments dedicated to him in 35 countries, Taras Shevchenko is arguably the most pivotal figure in Ukrainian literature and culture. But no memorial can compare with his symbolic role during the Revolution of Dignity. In 2014, Ukraine celebrated the 200th anniversary of the poet's birth, and activist Serhiy Nigoyan urged people to fight by invoking a fragment from Shevchenko's poem, The Caucasus, which became the rallying cry of the Maidan movement after Nigoyan's tragic death.

Born into serfdom, Taras Shevchenko experienced liberation at the age of 24, thanks to the recognition of his poetic talents by Karl Bryullov, a Russian artist and professor at the St. Petersburg Academy of Arts.

Eneida and Shevchenko's poetry are similar. Both highlight social issues and call for Ukrainians to fight for independence. Shevchenko's book of poems, Kobzar, is the cornerstone of Ukrainian literature, intertwining the simplicity of the poetic form with the theme of national liberation.

Did you know? Reportedly, Mykola Hohol, a Ukrainian writer, did not like Taras Shevchenko's simplistic style. However, some literary critics speculate that Hohol was jealous of Shevchenko's success in Ukraine, where Hohol failed to realize himself as an author.

chapters.title: Modernism helps people fall in love with Ukrainian culture
chapters.text:
Let's talk about western Ukraine, home to Ivan Franko, the son of a wealthy blacksmith and a woman from impoverished nobility. Franko became interested in journalism and later ventured into politics while studying at Lviv University. However, his social and political activities led to him being imprisoned by the Austrian authorities four times.

In 1890, Franko became a co-founder of the first Ukrainian political party. At that time, Lviv was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire and unsuccessfully ran for election.

After several attempts to enter politics, Franko eventually joined the Shevchenko Scientific Society and devoted himself to literature and science. He has written more than 500 poems, numerous prose texts, over a dozen dramas, and translations for renowned writers such as William Shakespeare, George Byron, Victor Hugo, Miguel de Cervantes, Dante Alighieri, and Adam Mickiewicz. Additionally, Franco has an extensive list of scientific works on the theory of literature and culture.

In the late 19th century, Ukrainian literature shifted from uplifting heroism to exploring modernism. Mykhailo Kotsiubynsky became one of the most characteristic representatives of modern prose. Kotsiubynsky's prose bears the same light, emotion, nature, and moments as great paintings by artists like Vincent van Gogh and Claude, but in text.

Imagine entering a natural setting after escaping a noisy city with many deadlines, problems, and worries — finally, a breath of fresh air. Mykhailo Kotsiubynsky conveys this sensation in his short story, Intermezzo.

In the small village of Hutsul region, Kryvorivnia, the writer found unity with nature, immersing himself in local life and folklore, crafting the tragic love story of Ivan and Marichka. This connection to life, which echoes in his masterpiece Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors, also resonates with Vasyl Stefanyk's texts. Living in the Ivano-Frankivsk region, Stefanyk masterfully recorded the lives of ordinary people. His novella, The Stone Cross, presents an intense portrayal of the pain experienced by peasants parting from their homeland.

Ukrainian/Armenian director Serhiy Paradzhanov filmed Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors — a sensual film with Hutsul songs and picturesque landscapes.

Did you know? During his performance at Eurovision 2022, Oleh Psiuk, the lead singer of the Ukrainian band Kalush Orchestra, wore a traditional Hutsul keptar (jacket) dating back to the early 20th century.

chapters.title: Olha Kobylianska and Lesya Ukrainka — modern feminism more than 100 years ago
chapters.text:
Lesya Ukrainka and Olha Kobylianska, master wordsmiths of Ukrainian literature, often accompanied Ukrainian writers on their visits to mountainous Kryvorivnia. Together they rested in Ivan Franko's house, discussed books, and listened to the roaring of the Cheremosh River while searching for inspiration. When they traveled around Ukraine, they continued to communicate via letters. Ukrainka enjoyed arguing with Franko, who often commented on her works.

Lesya Ukrainka serves as an inspiration to many Ukrainian women. Born into a literary family, she developed a love for books at an early age. Her uncle, the renowned literary critic and philosopher Mykhailo Drahomanov, significantly influenced her worldview and supported her as a critic and folklorist. From the tender age of nine, she started writing poetry and mastering various languages, enabling her to translate world classics. Despite contracting tuberculosis at ten, she recovered over time and produced a wealth of literary masterpieces.

Her poetic play The Forest Song captivated directors and readers alike, causing them to fall in love with Ukrainian mythology. Upon entering the forest, Perelesnyk, the alluring trickster, greets and guides you deeper into the wilderness, where the magical Mavka will enchant you to love her forever. In an enchanted trance, you will reach the lake, listen to the grumbling of the disgruntled Vodianyk (Water Goblin), and then Rusalka (Mermaid) will tickle you to death.

In such settings, Ukrainka tells the love story of Mavka and a man. There will be no happy ending, but there will be a rich universe of Ukrainian fairy tales.

Olha Kobylianska, a writer and close friend of Ukrainka, met her through their exchanged impressions and tender sisterly experiences via letters. Kobylianska was a little older than Ukrainka and advocated for women's independence, encouraging their constant self-development, education, and improvement. These women valued freedom, self-expression, support from other women, and opportunities for professional growth the most.

By reading the correspondence of Kobylianska and Ukrainka, you will see the ideas of modern feminism and human rights movements.

In her prose, Kobylianska explored life's difficulties through a woman's perspective, showing the contemporary intelligentsia's moral and ethical atmosphere and the village's socio-psychological problems.

To have such freedom to be your own goal! First, be a goal for yourself, work for your spirit like a bee; enrich it, increase it, and bring it to the point where it becomes radiant, beautiful, exciting, and shining in a thousand colors! ~ Olha Kobylianska

chapters.title: The Ukrainian 1930s — bohemian carefreeness and the dread of terror
chapters.text:
At the end of the 1920s, the government wanted to build a house for writers to live and create. Located in Kharkiv, the Slovo building had typewriters in each apartment, kindergartens, a dining room, a bomb shelter, rooftop solariums, and sound insulation. It was more than a luxury since Ukrainian writers usually lived in poverty, hiding manuscripts in pots from rats and making ends meet by writing in editorial offices.

The residents of the Slovo* building gradually paid off their apartment through their writer's fees. At the beginning of the last century, the coolest representatives of Ukrainian literature were neighbors, arranged public readings and parties, discussed each other's texts, experienced love and betrayal, and celebrated their youth and talent.

Among them, Mykola Khvylovy was the soul of the company. He often gathered with friends, played the guitar, sang, and initiated discussions that later became pamphlets. He supported the policy of Ukrainization, a cultural movement distancing itself from Moscow and embracing European influences. He also organized literary groups to unite authors with shared creative views.

Khvylovy considered himself a communist and sincerely believed it was possible to create a Ukrainian state with an independent culture within the borders of the Soviet Union. Later, he realized the inconsistency between revolutionary ideas and the methods used to achieve them. In his short story, I am (romance), he conveyed the horror of Bolshevik fanaticism, with the hero torn between duty and attachment to his mother.

However, these were Soviet times under Stalin's rule. He had a compulsive desire to structure society for easier population control.

Historians attribute the mass deaths during Stalin's reign to his paranoia of treason: the Soviet authorities imposed accusations of “ideological treason” and “damage.” Those arrested were exiled or executed.

The first arrests occurred a year after residents moved into the Slovo house. By 1938, over 40 apartment owners were arrested, and 33 were executed, most of whom were under the age of 40. This generation is called the Executed Renaissance.

In 1933, Khvylovy and his friend Arkady Liubchenko witnessed a devastating famine, a genocide later known as the Holodomor, in the villages of Poltava. Khvylovy telegraphed the Central Committee of the Communist Party about the tragedy. They replied that everything was going according to plan: to destroy the peasants' resistance by confiscating the grain.

Shortly after, Khvylovy's close friend, the writer Mykhailo Yalovy, was arrested. The following day, Khvylovy gathered with his friends to discuss the arrests and the possibility of securing his release. After the meeting, he went to study, and soon, his friends heard a shot. There was a suicide note on the table.

Yalovy's arrest is the execution of an entire Generation... For what? For us being the most sincere communists? I do not understand anything. ~ Mykola Khvylovy

Khvylovy's suicide became a protest action; historians associate it with the collapse of national communist ideology. The greatest tragedy of this generation lies not in the multiple arrests and deaths but in the sincere pursuit of an independent Ukraine within the Soviet Union, hoping for partial independence.

The Executed Renaissance delivers a profound lesson of the 20th century: there is no compromise with a tyrant. Conceding and sacrificing freedom will only result in losing everything.

* Learn more about the Slovo building in the summary of A Word About the Writers’ Home “Slovo.”

chapters.title: How Ivan Bahriany created a brand of Ukrainian courage
chapters.text:
By the end of the 1920s, the writer Ivan Bahriany visited various literary groups and published several poetry collections. However, in 1932, he was arrested for “counter-revolutionary agitation.” After spending several months in a solitary cell and years in exile, he wrote the novel The Hunters And The Hunted.

The main character escapes from a train transporting Gulag's death row prisoners. An NKVD major tries to capture him, but the freedom-loving hero will not surrender easily. By the 1950s, The Hunters And The Hunted was published in English, German, Spanish, and many other languages.

The Hunters And The Hunted is cherished worldwide as a birthday gift for teenagers, symbolizing the fight for justice and freedom.

In 1945, Bahriany emigrated to Germany, where he spent the rest of his life. He wrote the pamphlet Why I Am Not Going Back to The Soviet Union, condemning human rights violations and the Soviet genocide against its people. Later, he wrote the great novel Garden of Gethsemane, vividly describing the torture he experienced during NKVD imprisonment.

Did you know? In Kyiv, there are bars named after the writers — Hvylovy and Bahriany. Stop by and try Ukrainian craft beer, cider, or delicious coffee when you're in town.

chapters.title: Who is against tyranny — stand up! Ukrainian dissident movement
chapters.text:
The Soviet Union existed until 1991, and the fight against it continued until then. However, the consequences of totalitarianism and communism remain today. The Sixtiers, the generation of the 1960s, is most associated with the surge of the Ukrainian resistance movement. After the death of Joseph Stalin, Nikita Khrushchev came to power. This period is called The Khrushchev Thaw — a weakening of the totalitarian system.

The Sixtiers paid a lot of attention to the work of the Executed Renaissance and organized commemorative evenings dedicated to their repressed “literary fathers.” Many studies of Ukrainian modernism appeared, particularly by literary critics Ivan Dziuba, Yevhen Sverstiuk, and Ivan Svitlychny.

The 1960s witnessed the heyday of Ukrainian poetry when a more inspirational verse replaced sensual lyricism with sharp criticism of the Soviet government and Russification. But Lina Kostenko, Vasyl Symonenko, Ivan Drach, Mykola Vingranovsky, and Vasyl Stus did not experience creative freedom for long, receiving much criticism starting in 1962. Some went underground and engaged in samizdat (“self-publishing”); others compromised with the authorities and continued working in the social realism genre.

Social realism is a unique phenomenon presented by Soviet authorities as a realistic depiction of reality, only better. Imagine that you live in George Orwell's 1984 and must express your admiration for a distorted world.

Social realism is a totalitarian utopia.

1965 marked a boiling point in the arrests of the Ukrainian intelligentsia. It was when Serhiy Paradzhanov’s film Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors premiered at the Ukraina cinema in Kyiv. After the director's words, literary critic Ivan Dziuba took the stage and discussed the arrests. Journalist Viacheslav Chornovil called on young people to protest political repression. Poet Vasyl Stus shouted that censorship forbade people from telling the truth. Ivan Dziuba recalled that after his words, a siren wailed in the hall, and soldiers of the internal military forces surrounded the cinema.

The Sixtiers became a generation of human rights defenders. Their activities inspired the future generation of the eighties and those who had already written in independent Ukraine.

Did you know? Director Serhiy Paradzhanov’s surname is Paradzhanyan. But in the Soviet Union, building a career and having a national surname was difficult and dangerous. However, a change of surname did not save him from arrests and accusations of speculation and “homosexuality.”

chapters.title: Carnival is also a protest
chapters.text:
In 1985, three young poets created the Bu-Ba-Bu poetry group in Lviv. Yurii Andrukhovych, Viktor Neborak, and Oleksandr Irvanets even founded a festival and an award. In their work, the poets tried to make a carnival, entertain the audience, and create an alternative reality to criticize the late Soviet one. With the help of laughter, they resorted to reflecting on the empire's collapse.

Andrukhovych published his first novel, Recreations, toward the end of the '90s, featuring characters reminiscent of the Bu-Ba-Bu members. Together they travel through the carnival of phantasmagoria. Andrukhovych tried inventing a new Ukrainian language to revive the literary tradition in his prose.

Andrukhovych's novels convey the feeling of chaos and the search for identifying characteristics of the Eastern Bloc after the collapse of the Soviet Union.

The ‘90s in Ukraine brought uncertainty, a thirst for change, and self-irony. Bu-Ba-Bu tried to move away from the past, loudly slamming the door. They did not know what painful correction work would await Ukrainians in the future.

chapters.title: Hold on to what's yours
chapters.text:
Oksana Zabuzhko, another prominent figure in Ukrainian literature, worked alongside Andruhovych to find a new language and identity for the country. Born to a family of literary experts, she received philological education early. Subsequently, she devoted herself to analyzing Ukrainian literature in essays and journalism.

Her prose debut, the novel Fieldwork in Ukrainian Sex, sparked a lively discussion about female sexuality, national and gender identity, and sexual violence. The main character is a polyphonic woman who combines the images of a poet, an intellectual, a witch, and a harlot. Despite the love storyline, she remains equally individual, in eternal search for herself.

In Zabuzhko's texts, one can discern a characteristic accumulation of thoughts flowing in long, expressive sentences. Her prose evokes emotions but reads more like a critical essay.

By the late ‘90s, a voice emerged that would later resound the loudest. Serhii Zhadan began as a poet, combining humor and brutality in his poems. Eventually, he found his characteristic rhythm. Once you hear him reading his poetry, its melody will linger in your mind.

In 2004, he made his prose debut with the novel Depeche Mode. However, it was his work Voroshilovgrad that garnered widespread recognition. The story's mood is reminiscent of Jack Kerouac's prose. The reality of the Ukrainian East becomes poetic and sometimes magical. After a series of random events and strange coincidences, the hero returns to his hometown to win against the bandits.

At the beginning of Voroshilovgrad, the main character appears as a loser without a family and tribe. Yet, ultimately, he builds life guidelines and realizes the need to fight for his past.

In addition to poetic and prose work, Serhii Zhadan created his musical rock band Zhadan and Dogs, hosts a podcast, and regularly writes social and political essays. He also has experience writing librettos for operas and plays and even drew illustrations for a book of poems by the Ukrainian artist Oleksandr Roitburd.

chapters.title: Involve young people, build cultural diplomacy
chapters.text:
Simultaneously searching for a new literary language, Ukrainian literature actively expanded in the 2000s and early 2010s. Young people became interested in modern Ukrainian literature.

Yurii Vynnychuk is one of the oldest writers in the historical and erotic genres. In his prose, the Austro-Hungarian myth of Lviv comes to life, reinforced by local legends. Tango of Death describes Lviv during World War II. The storyline of the four friends diverges and intersects continuously, maintaining the most valuable thing - friendship. The destinies of a Pole, a Ukrainian, a German, and a Jew are different but all equally fascinating.

Andrii Kurkov remains the most popular contemporary Ukrainian writer abroad. His surreal novel, Death and the Penguin, became a bestseller and was translated into more than 30 languages. The adventures of the journalist and his penguin are full of black humor and satire, which helped them survive in the post-communist space of Ukraine in the late 90s.

In Kurkov's 2018 bestseller, “Grey Bees,” he explores the “gray zone” of the Russian-Ukrainian war. Former classmates are left alone in their native village, where they must reconcile, adapt to the political climate, and save the bees.

In addition to writing, Andrii Kurkov is actively engaged in cultural diplomacy. Since 1988, he has been a member of the English PEN club, and in 2018 he became the president of the Ukrainian PEN.

Tania Maliarchuk, another master of surrealist prose, moved to Vienna in 2011. She regularly creates texts for Deutsche Welle, continues to write in Ukrainian, and is a valid member of the Ukrainian PEN. She received the Ingeborg Bachman Award for her short story Frösche im Meer (Frogs in the Sea), where she talks about the gap between different generations. Her novel A Biography of a Chance Miracle tells the story of Lena, a girl trying to find her place in western Ukraine's “absurd” world.

Sofia Andrukhovych offers an alternative interpretation of the Austro-Hungarian myth of western Ukraine. Although her father, Yurii Andrukhovych, prefers burlesque and carnival reality, Sofia immerses herself in the psychology of her characters. The female characters of the novel Felix Austria lived in Stanislaviv (now Ivano-Frankivsk) at the beginning of the last century, and the two friends gradually turned their friendship into codependency. Sofia Andrukhovych masterfully conveys the Galician atmosphere, focusing on local gastronomy.

Literary festivals play an important role in creating a reading community, particularly the largest — Book Forum in Lviv and Book Arsenal in Kyiv. Ukraine has representation at international fairs thanks to the work of the Ukrainian Book Institute.

More than 100 Ukrainian book titles are published yearly in English, German, Polish, and other languages.

chapters.title: Instead of a conclusion. Light replaces darkness
chapters.text:
The Ukrainian book market is divided into “before” and “after” 2014. The Revolution of Dignity began as a peaceful student protest in November 2013. In February 2014, Russia launched a Crimean invasion, and in April of the same year, it tried to occupy the territories of Luhansk and Donetsk. These events caused many processes, including those in Ukrainian cultural policy. The market began to differentiate itself from the Russian one, displacing the cultural product of the aggressor state.

Ukrainian writers have collected a considerable publishing portfolio of reflections on everything happening during the ninth year of the Russian-Ukrainian war. This list is just a fraction of it, but many have been translated abroad:

• Mondegreen by Volodymyr Rafeienko is an understanding of the transition from Russian to Ukrainian by a writer from Donetsk. It's an attempt to come to terms with a foreign environment through the lens of linguistic research.
• In Isolation by Stanislav Aseyev is a story about 28 months of survival in the Isolation concentration camp in occupied Donetsk. Aseyev published the book after being released from captivity.
• Daughter by Tamara Duda is the debut novel of a Ukrainian volunteer. It is an artistic story about the transformation of a girl who lost everything during the war in Donbas and eventually became a warrior.
• How War Changed Rondo by Romana Romanyshyn and Andrii Lesiv, published by the Agrafka Creative Association, won international awards. This illustrated book explains war to children without taking away a bright sense of hope.
• Life Went on Anyway: Stories by Oleg Sentsov is a collection of autobiographical stories by the Ukrainian director and public activist. Sentsov was in Russian captivity from 2014 to 2019 and went on a hunger strike, demanding the release of Ukrainian political prisoners.

Although this trip was long, you've only seen the tip of the Ukrainian literary iceberg. You can always dive deeper into modernist experiments, discover rich Ukrainian mythology, or explore philosophical interpretations. Embrace the journey through Ukraine's history and culture because you can always learn something new.

Try this

• Once you realize the value of your past, you want to build your future. Remember that Ukrainians have always chosen to fight rather than surrender. Find something to hold on to, and you will always be brave.

Ukrainian Fiction 101

• Eneida by Ivan Kotliarevsky
• Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors by Mykhailo Kotsiubynskyi
• The Forest Song by Lesya Ukrainka
• The Hunters And The Hunted by Ivan Bahriany
• Voroshilovgrad by Serhii Zhadan
• Felix Austria by Sofia Andruhovych

Read it till the end?
